name: Atef Crown
description: The Atef Crown is a distinctive headdress worn by the ancient Egyptian god Osiris. It is a tall, white hedjet (the crown of Upper Egypt) flanked by two ostrich feathers. The crown often features a golden sun disk at its base and sometimes includes ram horns. The white color of the hedjet symbolizes Upper Egypt and purity, while the ostrich feathers represent truth, justice, and balance (Ma'at). The sun disk connects the crown to Ra, the sun god, and the ram horns symbolize strength and fertility. As Osiris is the god of the afterlife, resurrection, and fertility, the Atef Crown symbolizes his power and dominion over these realms. Wearing the Atef Crown signifies Osiris's role as the king of the afterlife and his connection to the cyclical nature of death and rebirth. It represents not only royal authority but also spiritual power and the promise of regeneration. The Atef crown reinforces Osiris's association with agriculture, fertility, and the renewal of life, crucial aspects of Egyptian belief and societal stability. The crown's components all contribute to its potent symbolism, making it a deeply significant item in Egyptian mythology and iconography. It is frequently depicted in funerary art and temple reliefs to ensure the deceased's successful passage into the afterlife, guided and protected by Osiris.
subtitle: Osiris's Crown of Resurrection
origin: Divine Creation associated with Osiris's kingship
currentLocation: Lost to time; representations exist in museums

keyMyths.title: Osiris's Reign
keyMyths.description: Osiris wore the Atef crown as a symbol of his righteous rule over Egypt before his murder by Set. The crown represented his authority and connection to the land's prosperity.

keyMyths.title: Osiris in the Underworld
keyMyths.description: After his death and resurrection, Osiris continued to wear the Atef crown as the king of the underworld. The crown symbolized his victory over death and his promise of eternal life.

sources.author: Plutarch
sources.description: Classical account of the Osiris myth.
sources.text: Classical account of the Osiris myth.
